message.content: Does 'ifrit' bottle install the claude code NPM?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.description: Search claude/npm references in ifrit vessel contexts

content.content:
# Ifrit (tether variant) — in-bottle attack binary for Recipe Bottle crucible testing
#
# Single-stage build: Rust toolchain stays in image (test vessel, size
# irrelevant, iteration speed matters). Layer-cached dependency compilation
# means source-only changes rebuild just the ifrit crate.
#
# Build context: rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/ (shared between tether and
# airgap variants — companion Dockerfile.airgap builds FROM a pre-warmed forge
# image to satisfy airgap egress discipline).

ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Avoid prompts from apt
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Install network tools for security boundary probes
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    socat           `# Echo service on port 8888 (entry port security tests)`  \
    dnsutils        `# dig/nslookup for DNS security tests`                    \
    netcat-openbsd  `# nc for TCP connectivity tests`                          \
    traceroute      `# ICMP path verification tests`                           \
    strace          `# Syscall boundary mapping from inside the container`     \
    libcap2-bin     `# setcap for CAP_NET_RAW file capability on ifrit binary` \
    curl            `# HTTP client for ad-hoc connectivity diagnosis`          \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Layer-cached dependency build: copy manifests first, dummy compile
WORKDIR /build
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs && \
    cargo build --release && \
    rm -rf src target/release/deps/rbid-* target/release/.fingerprint/rbid-*

# Real source build
COPY src/ src/
RUN cargo build --release

# Install binary to standard location with CAP_NET_RAW file capability.
# Container cap_add only populates the bounding set; non-root ifrit user
# needs file capabilities to get NET_RAW in effective set for raw sockets.
RUN cp target/release/rbid /usr/local/bin/rbid && \
    setcap cap_net_raw+ep /usr/local/bin/rbid

# Create unprivileged ifrit user
RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash ifrit

# Keep the container alive with echo service on port 8888
CMD ["socat", "TCP-LISTEN:8888,fork,reuseaddr", "EXEC:cat"]

toolUseResult.stdout:

COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs && \

RUN cargo build --release

586	  zrbob_sentinel

588	  exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" "$@"
589	}
590	
591	# Launch Claude Code client inside bottle (ifrit engagement)

596	}
597	
598	# Run ifrit sortie adjutant inside bottle (security test dispatch)
599	rbob_ifrit_sortie() {

602	  ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec -w "${RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE}" "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" \

606	######################################################################
607	# Drive Hallmark — rewrite a single RBRN_*_HALLMARK line in nameplate env
608	# Args: nameplate_file variable_name new_hallmark
609	
610	zrbob_drive_hallmark() {
611	  local -r z_file="$1"
612	  local -r z_var_name="$2"
613	  local -r z_new_value="$3"
614	
615	  test -f "${z_file}" || buc_die "Nameplate file not found: ${z_file}"
616	  test -n "${z_var_name}" || buc_die "Variable name required"
617	  test -n "${z_new_value}" || buc_die "New hallmark value required"
618	
619	  # Load file into array (BCG load-then-iterate)
620	  local z_lines=()
621	  while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
622	    z_lines+=("${z_line}")
623	  done < "${z_file}"
624	
625	  # Rewrite with substitution
626	  local -r z_temp="${ZRBOB_DRIVE_PREFIX}${z_var_name}"
627	  : > "${z_temp}" || buc_die "Failed to create temp file: ${z_temp}"
628	
629	  local z_found=0
630	  local z_i="" z_current=""
631	  for z_i in "${!z_lines[@]}"; do
632	    z_current="${z_lines[$z_i]}"
633	    case "${z_current}" in
634	      "${z_var_name}"=*)
635	        z_current="${z_var_name}=${z_new_value}"
636	        z_found=1
637	        ;;
638	    esac
639	    printf '%s\n' "${z_current}" >> "${z_temp}" || buc_die "Failed to write line"
640	  done
641	
642	  test "${z_found}" = "1" || buc_die "Variable ${z_var_name} not found in ${z_file}"
643	
644	  # Atomic replace

file.content:
rbob_bark() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  buc_step "Bark to bottle: ${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}"
  exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" "$@"
}

# Launch Claude Code client inside bottle (ifrit engagement)
rbob_ifrit_client() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  buc_step "Launching Claude Code client in bottle: ${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}"
  exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec -it -w "${RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE}" "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" claude
}

# Run ifrit sortie adjutant inside bottle (security test dispatch)
rbob_ifrit_sortie() {
  zrbob_sentinel
  buc_step "Running ifrit sortie adjutant in bottle: ${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}"
  ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec -w "${RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE}" "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" \
    python3 rbtia_adjutant.py "$@"
}

######################################################################
# Drive Hallmark — rewrite a single RBRN_*_HALLMARK line in nameplate env
# Args: nameplate_file variable_name new_hallmark

zrbob_drive_hallmark() {
  local -r z_file="$1"
  local -r z_var_name="$2"
  local -r z_new_value="$3"

  test -f "${z_file}" || buc_die "Nameplate file not found: ${z_file}"
  test -n "${z_var_name}" || buc_die "Variable name required"
  test -n "${z_new_value}" || buc_die "New hallmark value required"

  # Load file into array (BCG load-then-iterate)
  local z_lines=()
  while IFS= read -r z_line || test -n "${z_line}"; do
    z_lines+=("${z_line}")
  done < "${z_file}"

  # Rewrite with substitution
  local -r z_temp="${ZRBOB_DRIVE_PREFIX}${z_var_name}"
  : > "${z_temp}" || buc_die "Failed to create temp file: ${z_temp}"

  local z_found=0
  local z_i="" z_current=""
  for z_i in "${!z_lines[@]}"; do
    z_current="${z_lines[$z_i]}"
    case "${z_current}" in
      "${z_var_name}"=*)
        z_current="${z_var_name}=${z_new_value}"
        z_found=1
        ;;
    esac
    printf '%s\n' "${z_current}" >> "${z_temp}" || buc_die "Failed to write line"
  done

  test "${z_found}" = "1" || buc_die "Variable ${z_var_name} not found in ${z_file}"

  # Atomic replace

content.content:
80	The Ifrit ships as a three-vessel family under `rbev-vessels/`:
81	
82	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` — Tether-built variant for daily kludge iteration. Built directly from upstream Debian + cargo.
83	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` — Airgap-built variant produced from enshrined bases without upstream pulls. Same runtime image as the tether variant; differs only in supply-chain provenance.
84	* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` — Build-time fixture vessel that warms the cargo cache and pre-stages apt deps so the airgap build has no remote dependencies. Consumed input, not a runtime image.
85	
86	The two runtime variants share their source tree and Dockerfile family in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/` (`Dockerfile.tether` builds against the tether pool; `Dockerfile.airgap` uses `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` as `FROM` base). The fixture vessel itself lives in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/`.
87	
88	The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing:
89	
90	[cols="1,3"]
91	|===
92	| Package | Function
93	
94	| `nodejs` + `npm`
95	| Claude Code runtime (Claude Code is a Node.js application)
96	
97	| `python3-scapy`
98	| Arbitrary packet construction, raw socket access, protocol-level probing.
99	Scapy is the single most dangerous tool for a creative attacker — it exposes every capability that dedicated tools like nmap, hping3, and iodine provide, but programmatically, allowing an AI agent to craft and iterate on attacks at machine speed.
100	
101	| `strace`
102	| Syscall boundary mapping — lets the agent observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
103	Hard to replicate in pure Python.
104	
105	| `bash`, `curl`, `git`
106	| Claude Code operational dependencies
107	|===
108	
109	The tether variant of the Ifrit bottle is the `tadmor` nameplate's bottle vessel for daily-iteration adversarial validation against locally-kludged hallmarks.
110	The airgap variant is the `moriah` nameplate's bottle vessel, exercising the same security suite against hallmarks ordained end-to-end on the airgap pool — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs.
111	Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: connection to Anthropic API via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist, with no general egress.
112	First release is Anthropic-only; future releases may open access to additional LLM providers.
113	
114	==== Volume Mount Design
115	
116	The Ifrit bottle operates with two mount points:
117	
118	[cols="1,1,3"]
119	|===

file.content:
The Ifrit ships as a three-vessel family under `rbev-vessels/`:

* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-tether` — Tether-built variant for daily kludge iteration. Built directly from upstream Debian + cargo.
* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-airgap` — Airgap-built variant produced from enshrined bases without upstream pulls. Same runtime image as the tether variant; differs only in supply-chain provenance.
* `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` — Build-time fixture vessel that warms the cargo cache and pre-stages apt deps so the airgap build has no remote dependencies. Consumed input, not a runtime image.

The two runtime variants share their source tree and Dockerfile family in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/` (`Dockerfile.tether` builds against the tether pool; `Dockerfile.airgap` uses `rbev-bottle-ifrit-forge` as `FROM` base). The fixture vessel itself lives in `rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/`.

The Ifrit bottle is purpose-built for adversarial testing:

[cols="1,3"]
|===
| Package | Function

| `nodejs` + `npm`
| Claude Code runtime (Claude Code is a Node.js application)

| `python3-scapy`
| Arbitrary packet construction, raw socket access, protocol-level probing.
Scapy is the single most dangerous tool for a creative attacker — it exposes every capability that dedicated tools like nmap, hping3, and iodine provide, but programmatically, allowing an AI agent to craft and iterate on attacks at machine speed.

| `strace`
| Syscall boundary mapping — lets the agent observe what the kernel allows and denies from inside the container.
Hard to replicate in pure Python.

| `bash`, `curl`, `git`
| Claude Code operational dependencies
|===

The tether variant of the Ifrit bottle is the `tadmor` nameplate's bottle vessel for daily-iteration adversarial validation against locally-kludged hallmarks.
The airgap variant is the `moriah` nameplate's bottle vessel, exercising the same security suite against hallmarks ordained end-to-end on the airgap pool — the cloud-built variant validating that containment holds identically when the supply chain produces the inputs.
Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: connection to Anthropic API via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist, with no general egress.
First release is Anthropic-only; future releases may open access to additional LLM providers.

==== Volume Mount Design

The Ifrit bottle operates with two mount points:

[cols="1,1,3"]
|===

input.command: echo "=== airgap ==="; cat rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/common-ifrit-context/Dockerfile.airgap; echo; echo "=== forge ==="; cat rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/Dockerfile

content.content:
=== airgap ===
# Ifrit (airgap variant) — in-bottle attack binary for Recipe Bottle crucible testing
#
# Builds FROM the enshrined forge image which pre-stages all apt packages and
# a warm cargo dependency cache. This build needs zero network access — only
# the project's own rbid crate compiles.
#
# Build context: rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/ (shared with tether variant)

ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Real source build (dependency cache warm from forge — only rbid compiles)
WORKDIR /build
COPY src/ src/
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN cargo build --release

# Install binary to standard location with CAP_NET_RAW file capability.
# Container cap_add only populates the bounding set; non-root ifrit user
# needs file capabilities to get NET_RAW in effective set for raw sockets.
RUN cp target/release/rbid /usr/local/bin/rbid && \
    setcap cap_net_raw+ep /usr/local/bin/rbid

# Create unprivileged ifrit user
RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash ifrit

# Keep the container alive with echo service on port 8888
CMD ["socat", "TCP-LISTEN:8888,fork,reuseaddr", "EXEC:cat"]

=== forge ===
# Ifrit forge — pre-stages apt packages and warms cargo dependency cache
#
# Build-time fixture, not a runtime vessel. The forge image is conjured
# tethered, then enshrined into GAR as a base for the airgap ifrit build.
# By pre-staging all apt packages and compiled Rust dependencies, the
# airgap build needs zero network access — only project source compiles.
#
# Build context: rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/
# Cargo.toml + Cargo.lock must stay in sync with common-ifrit-context/.

ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Avoid prompts from apt
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Install network tools for security boundary probes (matches Dockerfile.tether)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    socat           `# Echo service on port 8888 (entry port security tests)`  \
    dnsutils        `# dig/nslookup for DNS security tests`                    \
    netcat-openbsd  `# nc for TCP connectivity tests`                          \
    traceroute      `# ICMP path verification tests`                           \
    strace          `# Syscall boundary mapping from inside the container`     \
    libcap2-bin     `# setcap for CAP_NET_RAW file capability on ifrit binary` \
    curl            `# HTTP client for ad-hoc connectivity diagnosis`          \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Layer-cached dependency build: copy manifests first, dummy compile
WORKDIR /build
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs && \
    cargo build --release && \
    rm -rf src target/release/deps/rbid-* target/release/.fingerprint/rbid-*

#

# a warm cargo dependency cache. This build needs zero network access — only
# the project's own rbid crate compiles.
#
# Build context: rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-context/ (shared with tether variant)

ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Real source build (dependency cache warm from forge — only rbid compiles)
WORKDIR /build
COPY src/ src/
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN cargo build --release

# Install binary to standard location with CAP_NET_RAW file capability.
# Container cap_add only populates the bounding set; non-root ifrit user
# needs file capabilities to get NET_RAW in effective set for raw sockets.
RUN cp target/release/rbid /usr/local/bin/rbid && \
    setcap cap_net_raw+ep /usr/local/bin/rbid

# Create unprivileged ifrit user
RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash ifrit

# Keep the container alive with echo service on port 8888
CMD ["socat", "TCP-LISTEN:8888,fork,reuseaddr", "EXEC:cat"]

=== forge ===
# Ifrit forge — pre-stages apt packages and warms cargo dependency cache
#
# Build-time fixture, not a runtime vessel. The forge image is conjured
# tethered, then enshrined into GAR as a base for the airgap ifrit build.
# By pre-staging all apt packages and compiled Rust dependencies, the
# airgap build needs zero network access — only project source compiles.
#
# Build context: rbev-vessels/common-ifrit-forge-context/
# Cargo.toml + Cargo.lock must stay in sync with common-ifrit-context/.

ARG RBF_IMAGE_1
FROM ${RBF_IMAGE_1}

# Avoid prompts from apt
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Install network tools for security boundary probes (matches Dockerfile.tether)
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
    socat           `# Echo service on port 8888 (entry port security tests)`  \
    dnsutils        `# dig/nslookup for DNS security tests`                    \
    netcat-openbsd  `# nc for TCP connectivity tests`                          \
    traceroute      `# ICMP path verification tests`                           \
    strace          `# Syscall boundary mapping from inside the container`     \
    libcap2-bin     `# setcap for CAP_NET_RAW file capability on ifrit binary` \
    curl            `# HTTP client for ad-hoc connectivity diagnosis`          \
    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

# Layer-cached dependency build: copy manifests first, dummy compile
WORKDIR /build
COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
RUN mkdir src && echo 'fn main() {}' > src/main.rs && \
    cargo build --release && \
    rm -rf src target/release/deps/rbid-* target/release/.fingerprint/rbid-*

content.text:
No — the ifrit bottle as actually built does **not** install Claude Code or its npm runtime. There's a spec/implementation drift here worth flagging.

**What the Dockerfiles actually install** (all three — `Dockerfile.tether`, `Dockerfile.airgap`, and the forge `Dockerfile` at `rbmm_moorings/rbmv_vessels/common-ifrit-context/` and `common-ifrit-forge-context/`):
- The `rbid` Rust attack binary (with `cap_net_raw+ep`)
- `socat`, `dnsutils`, `netcat-openbsd`, `traceroute`, `strace`, `libcap2-bin`, `curl`

No `nodejs`, no `npm`, no `@anthropic-ai/claude-code`.

**What the spec says should be there** — RBSIP (`Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc:94`) lists `nodejs` + `npm` as the "Claude Code runtime," plus `python3-scapy`, `bash`, `curl`, `git` as Claude Code operational dependencies. None of the scapy/node/git packages appear in the current Dockerfiles either.

**The launcher assumes it's present** — `rbob_ifrit_client()` (`Tools/rbk/rbob_bottle.sh:592`) does:
```
exec ${ZRBOB_RUNTIME} exec -it -w "${RBRR_BOTTLE_WORKSPACE}" "${ZRBOB_BOTTLE}" claude
```
That `claude` invocation would fail in a bottle built straight from these Dockerfiles, since nothing puts `claude` (or node) on PATH.

So the answer to your literal question is **no**. If you expected it to — the spec agrees with you, and the Dockerfiles have drifted away from it (the FROM base may carry node, but nothing in the ifrit build layers installs it). Want me to check what the `RBF_IMAGE_1` base contributes, or reconcile the Dockerfiles back to the RBSIP package list?

message.content: Do you agree that  the spec needs to be repaired?  I don't want claude code in the ifrit anymore.  Used to be.

content.text: Yes, I agree — if Claude Code is intentionally gone from the ifrit, then the Dockerfiles are right and the spec is the stale artifact. Let me map the full blast radius before you decide scope, because it's more than one table row.

97:| `python3-scapy`

106:| Claude Code operational dependencies
111:Both nameplates share the same restrictive network policy: connection to Anthropic API via the nameplate's DNS and CIDR allowlist, with no general egress.

lastPrompt: Do you agree that  the spec needs to be repaired?  I don't want claude code in the ifrit anymore.  Used to be.

content.content:
1	// RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc
2	// Ifrit Pentester — adversarial AI escape testing framework
3	//
4	// Included from RBS0-SpecTop.adoc under System Overview / Adversarial Validation
5	
6	// ============================================================================
7	// SYSTEM CONCEPT
8	// ============================================================================
9	
10	The {at_sentry_container} promises network containment.
11	The Ifrit exists to break that promise.
12	
13	An Ifrit is an adversarial AI agent running inside a {at_bottle_container} with:
14	
15	* **Perfect information**: read-only access to the entire {at_recipe_bottle} repository, including sentry Dockerfiles, iptables configuration scripts, nameplate definitions, and this specification
16	* **Programmatic attack tooling**: Python 3 with scapy (arbitrary packet construction), strace (syscall boundary mapping)
17	* **Persistence**: read-write access to an escape test directory where attack scripts and results accumulate across sessions
18	* **API connectivity**: network access to Anthropic API for Claude Code operation within the bottle
19	
20	The Ifrit is the strongest adversary short of root on the host: an intelligent agent with full knowledge of its prison, tooling to probe every seam, and the ability to iterate.
21	Every {at_nameplate} security test that passes in the presence of an Ifrit is evidence the containment holds.
22	The Ifrit campaign does not promise certainty — it validates the sentry's claims under adversarial pressure.
23	
24	==== Motivation
25	
26	Black-box security tests (the existing tadmor bash test suite) validate that specific known attack vectors are blocked.
27	They cannot discover what they do not anticipate.
28	An AI agent with packet-crafting capability and source-code-level understanding of the sentry can explore attack surfaces that static test suites miss: DNS exfiltration through dnsmasq, ICMP covert channels, direct attacks against the sentry container, protocol-level tricks to bypass port floor restrictions, and creative combinations thereof.
29	
30	The Ifrit approach treats security validation as an ongoing adversarial campaign, not a one-time checklist.

content.text:
Agreed, and it's deeper than I first framed it — this isn't a stale table row, it's a stale *premise*. Three tiers:

**Tier 1 — package lists (mechanical).** RBSIP:94-95 (`nodejs`+`npm` = "Claude Code runtime") and :106 ("Claude Code operational dependencies"). Straightforward deletions. Note `python3-scapy` (:16, :97) and `git` are also listed but not in the actual Dockerfiles — same drift, separate question of whether *those* should come back or the spec should drop them too.

**Tier 2 — the SYSTEM CONCEPT itself (conceptual).** The whole spec is built on the Ifrit *being* an AI agent: the title is "adversarial AI escape testing framework"; :13 defines an Ifrit as "an adversarial AI agent running inside a bottle"; :18 lists Anthropic API connectivity "for Claude Code operation"; :20 "an intelligent agent... ability to iterate"; :24-30 motivate the whole thing as AI-driven exploration vs. static tests. If Claude Code is out, the adversary is now the `rbid` Rust binary + the sortie adjutant (`rbtia_adjutant.py`) — *scripted* attacks, not an agent at the keyboard. That reframing touches the spec's spine, not its appendix.

**Tier 3 — live code that's now dead (not just docs).** The Claude-in-bottle behavior is also wired into shipping code:
- `rbob_ifrit_client()` (`rbob_bottle.sh:592`) execs `claude` in the bottle — fails now, nothing installs it.
- Zipper enrollment `RBZ_BOTTLE_IFRIT` → `rbw-Ic` (`rbz_zipper.sh:123`).
- Two real tabtargets: `tt/rbw-Ic.IfritClient.{tadmor,moriah}.sh`.
- Tabtarget-context doc row (`rbk-claude-tabtarget-context.md:99`).

So before I'd touch anything, two scope questions:

1. **Is the AI-agent framing retired entirely**, or do you want the spec to keep the *concept* of AI-driven testing as a future/external possibility while just removing claude from *this image*? (Determines whether Tier 2 is a rewrite or a hedge.)
2. **Does the `rbw-Ic` IfritClient path get ripped out** (launcher + zipper + tabtargets) since it's now a dead `claude` exec, or kept as a stub for launching some other interactive shell?

How do you want to bound it? This also feels like JJ heat work given the cross-cutting nature — want me to slate it rather than free-edit?

message.content:
1) Retired entirely from the ifrit, though I think we have a separate vessel that does this that is defined for kludging.
2) that actually does seem right too... but can you check for that other vessel that actually hosts a claude code?  Maybe I'm misremembering

content.content:
=== ccyolo Dockerfile (claude/node lines) ===
1:# ccyolo — Claude Code sandbox bottle for Recipe Bottle crucible
3:# SSH-accessible container running Claude Code. The sentry DNAT
5:# User SSHs in as "claude" (empty password, PAM-permissive),
6:# then runs `claude` to authenticate via OAuth copy/paste flow.
16:# Install SSH server and Claude Code operational dependencies
20:    curl              `# HTTP client (Claude Code operational dependency)`  \
21:    git               `# Version control (Claude Code operational dependency)` \
24:# Install Claude Code globally via npm (node and npm provided by base image)
27:RUN npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.89
29:# Create unprivileged claude user with empty password
30:RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash claude && \
31:    passwd -d claude
47:# Ensure PATH includes npm global bin for SSH sessions
50:# Preconfigure Claude Code for full autonomy (yolo mode).
53:RUN mkdir -p /home/claude/.claude && \
55:      > /home/claude/.claude/settings.json && \
56:    chown -R claude:claude /home/claude/.claude

=== rbrv.env (mode) ===
RBRV_SIGIL=rbev-bottle-ccyolo
RBRV_DESCRIPTION="Claude Code sandbox with SSH entry for network-contained AI development"
RBRV_USER=claude
RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=conjure
RBRV_EGRESS_MODE=tether
RBRV_RELIQUARY=r260518182400
RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${RBRV_SIGIL}/build-context/Dockerfile"
RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT="${RBRR_VESSEL_DIR}/${RBRV_SIGIL}/build-context"
RBRV_CONJURE_PLATFORMS="linux/amd64 linux/arm64"
RBRV_IMAGE_1_ORIGIN=node:22-slim

lastPrompt: 1) Retired entirely from the ifrit, though I think we have a separate vessel that does this that is defined for kludging. 2) that actually does seem right too... but can you check for that other vesse…

content.text:
Both recollections check out. Here's `rbev-bottle-ccyolo`:

- **Dockerfile** (`build-context/Dockerfile`): base `node:22-slim`, then `npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code@2.1.89` (pinned). Installs ssh + curl + git as "Claude Code operational dependencies."
- **Access model**: SSH in as user `claude` (empty password, PAM-permissive), then run `claude` to OAuth via copy/paste. The sentry DNATs the SSH port.
- **"yolo"**: `.claude/settings.json` preconfigured for full autonomy.
- **Purpose** (`rbrv.env`): *"Claude Code sandbox with SSH entry for network-contained AI development."* Even ships a buggy `count_words.sh` workspace fixture for "ask Claude to fix it."

One correction to your memory: it's `RBRV_VESSEL_MODE=conjure` (cloud-built), not kludge — though like any vessel it can be kludged locally. The "defined for a dev sandbox" instinct is right; it's just conjure-mode by default.

**This tightens the case for both points:**

1. **Retire from ifrit — confirmed clean.** The "interactive Claude in a contained bottle" capability has a proper, dedicated home in ccyolo. The ifrit doesn't need to double as that.

2. **Rip out `rbw-Ic` IfritClient — confirmed clean.** It's not just dead (`exec ... claude` with no claude installed) — it's *redundant*. ccyolo already delivers interactive-Claude-in-bottle via the SSH model (`rbw-cS SshTo`), which is the more honest transport anyway. So `rbob_ifrit_client()` + zipper enrollment + the two `rbw-Ic.IfritClient.{tadmor,moriah}.sh` tabtargets + the context-doc row can all go.

So the ifrit becomes purely scripted-adversary (`rbid` binary + sortie adjutant), and ccyolo owns the live-agent role. Clean separation.

This is genuinely cross-cutting — spec rewrite (Tier 2 conceptual reframe) + code/tabtarget/zipper removal + generated-doc regen. I'd slate it as JJ work rather than free-edit across all of it. Want me to mount/create a heat for it, or is there an existing heat this belongs under?

content: Goal: purge Claude Code from the ifrit vessel and its spec, leaving ccyolo as the dedicated Claude Code bottle. Confirmed ccyolo exists and the ifrit cleanup is clean. Next: slate this as a JJ heat or name the existing heat to use. (disable recaps in /config)

content.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BP

## Shape

Grab-bag cleanup heat. Loose theme: consolidate to the single rightful owner — remove capability and constants that are redundantly held in more than one place. Two strands so far, open to other post-cutover cleanups:

- **ifrit / ccyolo separation** — ifrit becomes purely scripted-adversary (rbid binary + sortie adjutant); ccyolo owns the live-agent role via its SSH model. Retire the redundant `rbw-Ic` IfritClient (dead — execs `claude` with no `claude` installed; redundant with `rbw-cS` SshTo, the more honest transport). Tier-2 spec reframe + code/tabtarget/zipper removal + generated-doc regen. (Authored in a separate session — slated there.)
- **cross-language constant projection** — replace hand-maintained bash↔Rust constant mirrors with build-time generation from the canonical bash source.

## Locked decisions

- **Gated on ₣BK (moorings cutover) closing.** The projection strand reads RBCC and the moorings layout; both are reshaped throughout ₣BK. No projection work starts until ₣BK retires — stable RBCC, settled layout, RBBC aliases gone. The ifrit strand is not gated but rides here by choice: one grab-bag over two heats.
- **Ordering: ifrit before codegen.** The ifrit strand removes `rbw-Ic` from the rbz registry; the codegen projects rbz. Clean the registry before generating its mirror.
- **Projection scope rule: single canonical bash author, or out.** Colophons (rbz) and RBCC flat tinder qualify. Distributed-identity classes (fixtures, operations, container roles — held across bash arrays, disk imprints, and Rust) are excluded unless a separate canonical-author decision is taken.

## What done looks like

- ifrit is scripted-adversary only; ccyolo owns the live-agent role; `rbw-Ic` and its enrollment/tabtargets are gone.
- The rbtd colophon mirror and RBCC-derived Rust constants are generated, checked in, regenerated before each Rust build, and gated by a diff check; the hand-maintained mirror and its runtime verify are retired.

## Out of scope

- Unifying fixture/operation/container-role identity (separate canonical-author decision; not pulled in unless deliberately scoped).
- Any work that depends on RBCC or the moorings layout before ₣BK closes.
---EOF---

content.text: Heat ₣BP already anticipates this — its paddock has the ifrit/ccyolo strand documented as the first of two cleanup strands, marked "(Authored in a separate session — slated there.)" but with no pace actually enrolled yet. The shape and locked decisions there match exactly what we landed on. Let me enroll the pace.

input.content:
# jjezs_slate ifrit-retire-claude-code

## Character
Cross-cutting removal: spec reframe requiring judgment, then mechanical code/registry deletion + regen.

Retire Claude Code from the ifrit entirely. The ifrit becomes a purely scripted
adversary (the `rbid` binary + the sortie adjutant); the live-agent role belongs
to `rbev-bottle-ccyolo`, which hosts `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` and is reached
via the SSH model (`rbw-cS`). The ifrit Dockerfiles already install no node/npm/
claude — this pace makes the spec and the launcher machinery match that reality.

Two parts:

- **Spec reframe (judgment).** `Tools/rbk/vov_veiled/RBSIP-ifrit_pentester.adoc`.
  The SYSTEM CONCEPT currently defines an Ifrit *as* an adversarial AI agent with
  Anthropic-API connectivity. Reframe to scripted-adversary; drop the Claude Code
  runtime package rows and the "API connectivity for Claude Code" framing. While
  there, reconcile the package-list drift: scapy and git are listed in the spec
  but absent from the actual Dockerfiles — decide at mount whether they return to
  the Dockerfiles or leave the spec (the sortie adjutant does run python3).

- **Rip `rbw-Ic` IfritClient (mechanical).** It's dead (`exec ... claude` with no
  claude installed) and redundant with `rbw-cS`. Remove the launcher, its zipper
  enrollment, the per-nameplate tabtargets, and the generated context-doc row.
  Discovery: `grep -rin "ifrit_client\|IfritClient\|rbw-Ic" Tools/rbk/ tt/`.
  Regenerate the tabtarget context via the marshal-generate path after removal.

## Done looks like
ifrit is scripted-adversary only; ccyolo owns the live-agent role; `rbw-Ic` and
its enrollment/tabtargets/doc-row are gone; the regenerated context reflects it.

content.text:
Slated as **₢BPAAA (ifrit-retire-claude-code)**, first pace in heat ₣BP (rbk-13-mvp-redundancy-cleanup).

The docket captures both halves:
- **Spec reframe** (judgment) — reframe RBSIP's SYSTEM CONCEPT from "adversarial AI agent" to scripted adversary, drop the Claude Code runtime rows, and reconcile the scapy/git package-list drift at mount.
- **Rip `rbw-Ic`** (mechanical) — launcher + zipper enrollment + tabtargets + generated doc-row, then regenerate the context.

It lands as `first` per the paddock's locked ordering ("ifrit before codegen"). It's also not gated on ₣BK — the paddock notes the ifrit strand rides in this heat by choice, not dependency, so it can mount anytime; only the codegen strand waits for the moorings cutover to close.

Nothing's been edited in the working tree — this is slate-only. Mount ₢BPAAA when you're ready to execute.
